pattern: Pairing each string with its exact reverse is a single left-to-right scan with a seen-set, since every string can be reversed and looked up in constant time.
hints: A word can only ever pair with its own reversed form, and since every word is distinct there is at most one possible partner for each word in the whole array.
Scan the words once, keeping a set of words seen so far; when the current word's reverse is already in that set, that's a new pair.
Count a match and add the current word to the seen set every time you scan one, regardless of whether it matched; the running count of matches is the answer.

edge_inputs.description: a single word, no partner possible
every word is a two-letter palindrome, none can pair since a word can't pair with a second copy of itself
the reverse of a word appears earlier in the array, not later, order must not matter
two independent reverse-pairs mixed together, both must be found
one word's true partner is present but a third word only superficially resembles a match without being the exact reverse
the maximum array length, built from 25 disjoint reverse-pairs so every word matches exactly one other

stress.description: a random sample of distinct two-letter words drawn from the full 26x26 alphabet
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    letters = string.ascii_lowercase
    all_words = [a + b for a in letters for b in letters]
    n = min(50, max(1, size))
    words = rng.sample(all_words, n)
    return [words]

stress.description: words constructed as disjoint reverse-pairs (no palindromic two-letter words), maximizing the number of matches
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    letters = string.ascii_lowercase
    candidates = [a + b for a in letters for b in letters if a != b]
    rng.shuffle(candidates)
    words = []
    used = set()
    limit = min(50, max(2, size))
    for w in candidates:
        if len(words) >= limit:
            break
        r = w[::-1]
        if w in used or r in used:
            continue
        words.append(w)
        words.append(r)
        used.add(w)
        used.add(r)
    words = words[:limit]
    return [words]
